reason: The paper fits ranger random-forest models over huge covariate stacks (hundreds of thousands of pixels, dozens of correlated remote-sensing/climate layers) and reports "Top ten most important covariates per nutrient, reported by the ranger package" (Table 2) and a "variable importance plot" (Fig. 11); nowhere does the text specify permutation, SHAP, conditional (party/cforest), or debiased importance, and the emphasis on computational feasibility ("MLA's can demand excessive computing time... limit prediction modeling to ranger and xgboost") is consistent with the cheaper default impurity/variance-reduction importance rather than the costlier permutation variant. The ranking is used to state central, assertive conclusions: "soil pH is clearly most important covariate for Na, K, Ca, Mg, Al," "Precipitation... distinctly comes as the most important covariate overall," and, for crop yield, "the most influential predictors... are: crop type, selection of nutrients and micro-nutrients (Mn, Zn, Al, B, Na)... This proves that producing maps of soil nutrients is indeed valuable for modeling agricultural productivity" -- the latter being effectively the paper's headline justification for its mapping effort. No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out validation of the selected feature set is reported anywhere; the only quantitative checks given (OOB RMSE, cross-validated R-square) validate overall predictive accuracy, not the covariate ranking itself. The authors' own hedge -- that "because soil nutrients are heavily cross-correlated... relatively high importance of Mn and Zn could also indicate high importance of P, B, Fe and/or S" -- is an informal acknowledgment of exactly the correlation/interaction confound that drives impurity-importance bias, but it is a caveat, not a corroborating analysis, and it leaves the confident core claim (soil-pH/precipitation/nutrient dominance) intact. All three CONFIRMED criteria are therefore met.
